name: prompt-library
description: Curated collection of high-quality prompts for various use cases. Includes role-based prompts, task-specific templates, and prompt refinement techniques. Use when user needs prompt templates, role-play prompts, or ready-to-use prompt examples for coding, writing, analysis, or creative tasks.
capabilities: Provides 20+ ready-to-use prompt templates across multiple categories
Includes role-based prompts for developer, reviewer, writer, and architect personas
Offers task-specific templates for debugging, refactoring, testing, and documentation
Contains analysis prompts for code complexity, performance, and security review
Teaches prompt engineering techniques like Chain of Thought and Few-Shot Learning
Includes a prompt improvement checklist for crafting better prompts
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer detected 53 patterns (external_commands, network URLs, C2 keywords, crypto algorithms). All findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the file is pure Markdown documentation containing prompt templates, not executable code. Backticks are code block delimiters, URLs are resource references, and flagged keywords appear in example text only. No actual security risks exist.
valueStatement: Stop crafting prompts from scratch. Get instant access to battle-tested templates for coding, documentation, analysis, and creative tasks.
limitations: Provides prompt templates only - does not execute code or perform actual analysis
Templates are generic and may need customization for specific projects
Does not include domain-specific prompts for specialized industries
Requires user to understand the context and adapt prompts appropriately

useCases.title: Developer seeking code review templates
useCases.description: A software engineer needs consistent code review feedback. Uses the Code Reviewer and Expert Developer prompts to get structured, actionable feedback on their pull requests.

useCases.title: Technical writer creating documentation
useCases.description: A documentation specialist uses the Technical Writer and API Documentation prompts to transform complex technical specs into clear, accessible user guides.

useCases.title: Team lead brainstorming product features
useCases.description: A product manager uses the Brainstorm Features and Name Generator prompts to facilitate ideation sessions and generate structured feature proposals.

promptTemplates.title: Beginner: Ask for Code Explanation
promptTemplates.prompt: Explain this code as if I'm new to programming. Break down what each part does, identify the main logic flow, and explain any patterns or concepts used. Include a simple analogy if helpful.
promptTemplates.scenario: You encountered unfamiliar code and need to understand it quickly

promptTemplates.title: Beginner: Debug With Guidance
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm getting this error: [paste error]. Here's my code: [paste code]. Help me understand what's causing this error and walk me through how to fix it step by step.
promptTemplates.scenario: You're stuck on a bug and need guided debugging help

promptTemplates.title: Intermediate: Code Review Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this code for bugs, performance issues, and code quality. Format your feedback as: Critical Issues (must fix), Suggestions (should consider), and Praise (what's done well). Provide specific code examples for each suggestion.
promptTemplates.scenario: You want structured, actionable feedback before merging code

promptTemplates.title: Advanced: System Architecture Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a system architecture for [describe requirements]. Consider scalability, reliability, maintainability, performance, and cost. Provide a component diagram description, data flow explanation, and trade-off analysis for key decisions. Include technology recommendations with justification.
promptTemplates.scenario: You need to design a new system or major feature from scratch

bestPractices: Always provide context and background information when using prompt templates
Customize templates with specific details about your project or use case
Iterate on prompts - refine based on the quality of responses you receive
antiPatterns: Using generic templates without adding specific context or requirements
Expecting identical results across different AI models without adjustment
Skipping the prompt improvement checklist and submitting vague requests

faq.question: How do I choose the right prompt template?
faq.answer: Match the template to your goal: use role-based prompts for persona-driven tasks, task-specific prompts for concrete objectives like debugging or testing, and analysis prompts for evaluation needs.

faq.question: Can I modify these prompts for my specific needs?
faq.answer: Yes, these templates are starting points. Customize them by adding your specific context, requirements, constraints, and examples of desired output.

faq.question: What makes a prompt effective?
faq.answer: Effective prompts are specific, provide context, define the desired format, include examples when possible, state constraints clearly, and describe what success looks like.

faq.question: Do these prompts work with all AI models?
faq.answer: Most prompts work across Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, but results may vary. More advanced models generally follow complex instructions better. Adjust complexity based on your model.

faq.question: How can I improve my prompt writing skills?
faq.answer: Study the prompt engineering techniques section, use the improvement checklist for each prompt, analyze what works well in responses, and iteratively refine your approach.

faq.question: What should I do if a prompt doesn't give good results?
faq.answer: Add more context, be more specific about your requirements, provide examples of desired output, or try a different template category that better matches your goal.
